entry.title: AI Comes to the Classroom: Updated Academic Lesson on SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
A Lesson That Keeps Pace with the Technology

If you have used the Academic Lesson on "Designing Stories with SAP Analytics Cloud" before, you already know it as a solid bridge between textbook concepts and real-world analytics tooling. The lesson walks students through data exploration in SAP Analytics Cloud, then into the design of impactful data stories, all with free access to SAP Learning Hub, student edition courses and practice systems.

The latest release, **24-sac-001d**, builds on that foundation with a meaningful addition: Activity 3: Applying AI-Powered Visualizations and Augmented Analytics to Business Data in SAP Analytics Cloud.

What Is New in This Update
A Third Activity Focused on AI and Augmented Analytics

The previous version (24-sac-002c, August 2025) included two activities:

Activity 1 - Exploring SAP Analytics Cloud (navigation, modeling, planning features)
Activity 2 - Designing Stories in SAP Analytics Cloud (building and presenting data-driven Stories)

This update adds:

Activity 3 - Applying AI-Powered Visualizations and Augmented Analytics to Business Data in SAP Analytics Cloud

Activity 3 draws from the SAP Learning Hub, student edition course of the same name and introduces students to two units of content:

1. Introducing AI, Machine Learning, and Augmented Analytics - foundational concepts on how AI and machine learning underpin modern business intelligence.
2. Using SAP Analytics Cloud Smart Features - hands-on work with Just Ask, the natural-language query feature that lets users type a question and receive an auto-generated visualization, and Smart Insights, which surfaces data patterns automatically.

Students complete the two units (approximately 52 minutes of course content), earn passing scores on the associated quizzes, and work through a practice exercise where they build a Just Ask Canvas Story live in SAP Analytics Cloud.

New Discussion Questions That Go Beyond the Tool

Activity 3 also comes with three discussion questions designed to engage student critical thinking:

  1. Responsible use of AI-generated visualizations: Students consider what happens when a manager asks them to drop a Just Ask chart into a quarterly business review. Who is responsible for accuracy? What should be verified before presenting AI-generated output?
  2. Data quality and forecasting: Students examine how flawed input data affects forecast reliability, connecting back to the "Garbage In, Garbage Out" principle from Activity 2.
  3. Audience-driven design with AI content: Students compare how a 12-month sales forecast story should be designed differently for an internal sales team versus executive leadership, including how to handle confidence intervals and the visual distinction between actuals and predictions.

These questions are ready to use as class discussions, group activities, essay prompts, or inter-teaching exercises. Suggested answers are included for instructors as a starting point of discussion.

Why This Update Matters for Your Classroom

The shift from specialist-driven analytics to business-user-driven analytics is one of the most significant trends in the data landscape. Organizations increasingly expect graduates, regardless of discipline, to be comfortable interpreting data and questioning AI-generated outputs. 

Here is what makes it practical for teaching

Low barrier to entry - Activity 3 requires only about 52 minutes of course content and one practice exercise. You can add it to an existing assignment without overhauling your syllabus.

No prerequisites beyond the lesson itself -  Students who complete Activities 1 and 2 have all the context they need. The lesson remains accessible to undergraduate and graduate students across business, information systems, social sciences, natural sciences, engineering, medicine, journalism, and beyond.

Free access - The SAP Learning Hub student edition, the courses, and the practice systems are all available at no cost to students and lecturers at institutions of higher education.

Real AI, not simulated -  Students interact with actual AI-powered features in SAP Analytics Cloud. They type natural-language questions, receive generated charts, and evaluate the results, it is hands-on experience with enterprise-grade augmented analytics.

Critical thinking built in - The discussion questions encourage students to treat AI as a starting point, not a finished product. That mindset, evaluating, refining, and contextualizing AI output is one of the most important skill students can develop right now.

How to Get Started

The updated lesson is available for download in two formats:

PDF Version: https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/u6XbMD1?rc=10&doi=SAP1200446](https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/u6XbMD1?rc=10&doi=SAP1200446
Word Version: https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/keWdTC4?rc=10&doi=SAP1191525](https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/keWdTC4?rc=10&doi=SAP1191525

The Word version is fully editable, so you can adapt the student instructions, deliverables, and discussion questions to fit your course design. Add requirements, remove what does not apply, and make it your own.

To access the courses and practice systems, students and lecturers will need an SAP Universal ID and enrollment in the SAP Learning Hub, student edition. If this is your first time using materials as part of the SAP University Alliances program, visit the Getting Started resources linked within the lesson document for setup guidance.

A Note on Flexibility

One of the strengths of this Academic Lesson has always been its adaptability. You do not have to assign all three Activities. You can assign any combination that fits your learning outcomes. You can use the discussion questions as graded essays, informal class discussions, or skip them entirely.

That said, if you are looking for one update to bring into your next offering, Activity 3 is a strong candidate. Giving students hands-on time with AI-augmented analytics, alongside the critical thinking prompts to evaluate what the AI produces, is a combination that serves them well regardless of where their careers take them.

entry.title: SAP Revenue Growth Management Integrated with SAP BDC: Seamless Data Integration & Governed Insights
entry.content:
1. Why CPG Organizations Struggle with Fragmented Data and Slow Insights 

Key account managers (KAMs), IT admins, and partners in the Consumer Products industry all wrestle with the same trio of issues: fragmented data across ERP, CRM, and market sources; long and fragile integrations for each new initiative; and analytics that take too long to trust, let alone act on.

The result is slower promotion cycles, delayed deductions and trade claims reconciliation, and AI projects that stall because the “single source of truth” never quite materializes. SAP Business Data Cloud (SAP BDC) exists to break that cycle—and when SAP Revenue Growth Management (RGM) integrates as part of the Consumer Products Intelligence solution on SAP BDC, the platform turns integration projects into a guided setup and makes governed, AI‑ready data the default state.

 

2. Key Concepts Explained: SAP BDC, Data Products & Intelligent Apps 

SAP Business Data Cloud (SAP BDC) is a fully managed SaaS solution that unifies and governs all SAP data and seamlessly connects with third-party data—enabling data management, analytics, and AI capabilities. It brings together the existing capabilities of SAP Datasphere, SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP Business Warehouse (BW) and incorporates SAP Databricks, line of business data products, and SAP’s open data ecosystem partners to create an integrated solution. 

Data Products (DPs) are curated, reusable datasets—packaged with metadata and APIs—so applications can consume or produce data without point‑to‑point integration. In this model, a producer is any application that creates and publishes data products into SAP BDC, while a consumer is any application or tool that reads those data products from SAP BDC to support analytics, AI, or operational processes. 

SAP Business Data Cloud Intelligent Applications are SAP‑managed apps built on certified data products that deliver embedded analytics and AI‑powered experiences out of the box.  

 

3. How it All Connects: Architecture Overview 

The diagram above illustrates the Consumer Products Intelligence solution on SAP Business Data Cloud—which includes SAP Revenue Growth Management. 

A CPG manufacturer uses SAP Revenue Growth Management to manage trade promotions. When a KAM creates a promotion within the application, SAP Revenue Growth Management consumes S/4HANA master data and sales- and finance-related data products via SAP BDC to power its core features, including promotion planning and embedded analytics. 

Once the promotion is approved and released, SAP Revenue Growth Management writes promotion data back to S/4HANA—supporting processes such as Claims and Condition Contract Management in SAP Revenue Growth Management. 

At the same time, SAP Revenue Growth Management produces its own data products back into SAP BDC, making them available to SAP Analytics Cloud. These dashboards help KAMs quickly view spend vs. uplift and spot which accounts in their territory delivered the strongest promotion ROI—so those winning promotions can be copied to drive future revenue growth. 

 

4. How SAP Revenue Growth Management on SAP BDC Delivers Value 

4.1. Automated and Seamless Integration Across SAP Systems 

Instead of wiring SAP Revenue Growth Management to each source system, users add the solution to an SAP BDC “formation” (a logical grouping of your SAP systems) in SAP for Me. The platform then handles the essential integration steps and installs the required data products for users—reducing manual setup and ambiguity. For example, SAP Revenue Growth Management automatically receives the S/4HANA data products it needs without manual setup.  

 

4.2. Harmonized SAP Data: Powered by Data Products and One Domain Model (ODM) 

SAP Revenue Growth Management's embedded analytics feature—five dashboards covering promotion performance, revenue, spend vs. uplift, volume, and profit and loss—has always been built into the application. With the SAP BDC integration, the underlying data powering these dashboards now flows through standardized, governed data products rather than point-to-point connections. Because all SAP data is exposed as standardized data products following SAP's One Domain Model (ODM), data across different SAP systems is harmonized by design—making it effortless to combine and analyze across systems. This lays the foundation for increasingly powerful analytics capabilities going forward, including customizable dashboards and broader cross‑system analysis. 

  

4.3. Extensibility: Unlocking Third-Party Data for a More Complete Picture 

The integration of SAP Revenue Growth Management with SAP BDC also opens the door for customers to enrich their analysis with data from outside the SAP ecosystem. As SAP BDC continues to evolve, customers will be able to import their own third-party data into SAP BDC in a standardized way, enabling it to flow into SAP Revenue Growth Management without custom pipelines or one-off integrations. 

For example, a beverage company will be able to import sell-out data—such as point-of-sale records from retailers showing actual consumer purchases—and combine it with promotion and account plan data directly within SAP Revenue Growth Management for scenario-based analysis. The result: a more complete picture of promotion effectiveness, grounded in both SAP data and real-world market signals. 

 

5. What’s New: SAP Revenue Growth Management on SAP BDC 

5.1. Streamlined Data Product Consumption Through End-to-End Provisioning Automation 

Guided Wizard in SAP for Me

Previously, provisioning required separate manual steps—creating a formation, configuring connectivity, and installing data products individually. Now, as an intelligent application, the Consumer Products Intelligence solution in SAP Business Data Cloud provides a guided wizard in SAP for Me that handles tenant creation, SAP BDC integration setup, and connectivity configuration—without IT admins needing to interpret long setup guides. 

Automatic Dependency Management

SAP Revenue Growth Management automatically consumes the SAP‑managed data products it depends on. IT admins and onboarding partners no longer need to do any manual work—all relevant dependencies are automatically identified, installed, and activated during the provisioning process. 

 

5.2. Standardized Data Product Production for Richer Insights 

Previously, accessing application data required custom API integrations and one-time development effort for each connection—costly to build and difficult to maintain. Now, as SAP Revenue Growth Management processes key business data such as promotion plans, baselines, actuals, and analytics, it produces its own standardized data products into SAP BDC. These data products share a harmonized data model with other SAP data products, enabling customers to conduct holistic analysis across their data landscape—and empowering agentic AI capabilities in Joule to deliver insightful suggestions and actions grounded in rich, connected business data. 

 

6. Join Our Co-Innovation Initiative 

The integration of SAP Revenue Growth Management with SAP Business Data Cloud marks a significant step toward faster setup, governed analytics, and connected insights for Consumer Products companies. 

By unifying inbound consumption of S/4HANA data products and outbound data products from SAP Revenue Growth Management for analytics and extension, we aim to help CPG companies stay agile and competitive in a rapidly changing market. 

If you’re an existing or prospective customer willing to explore these capabilities firsthand, you're welcome to join our co‑innovation program. Contact us at RGM_Engage@sap.com to start the journey toward modernizing trade promotion execution and unlocking governed insights across commercial processes. 

 

More Information

Product Page for SAP Revenue Growth Management 

Documentation on SAP Help Portal

Topic Page for SAP Revenue Growth Management

    • Introducing Calendar View in SAP Revenue Growth Management: A Visual Leap for Strategic Planning
    • Decoding Promotional Success: Embedded Analytics in SAP Revenue Growth Management
    • Unlocking Trade Spend Control Efficiency with Funds Management in SAP Revenue Growth Management
    • Consolidating Customer Planning: Unleash Growth with SAP Revenue Growth Management’s Account Plan
    • Elevate Your Promotion Strategy: Baseline Management with SAP Revenue Growth Management 
    • Driving Promotion Excellence: Streamlined Creation and Copying with SAP Revenue Growth Management
    • Financial Execution & Claims for Trade Promotions with SAP Revenue Growth Management & SAP Cloud ERP
    • Master Your KPIs: How SAP Revenue Growth Management Boosts Data-Driven Growth 
    • Modernize Planning: Universal Hierarchy in SAP Revenue Growth Management for SAP Cloud ERP Customers
    • Promotion Creation Reimagined: AI‑Assisted Single & Bulk Workflows in SAP Revenue Growth Management
    • One Plan, Multiple Measures: Driving Growth with Flexible Units in SAP Revenue Growth Management 
    • SAP Revenue Growth Management Integrated with SAP BDC: Seamless Data Integration & Governed Insights

entry.title: Bringing Analytics to Life with Business Builders – The Hockey Analyst
entry.content:
For more than 15 years, Université de Sherbrooke has been a member of the SAP University Alliances program, and our School of Management has continuously benefited from this global academic community to innovate in the teaching of enterprise systems. Over the years, SAP technologies have allowed us to move beyond theory and immerse students in realistic, hands-on learning experiences. With the launch of Business Builders – The Hockey Analyst, this pedagogical journey has taken an exciting new turn, one that immediately resonated with our students.

From the classroom to the rink: a natural fit

When we first introduced Business Builders – The Hockey Analyst in class, one of the most striking aspects was how easy it was to deploy. As a free, cloud-based solution built on SAP Analytics Cloud, the game required no complex setup, no local installations, and no steep learning curve. Within a single class session, students were able to log in, explore dashboards, and begin analyzing data. This simplicity made it easy to integrate the game seamlessly into existing courses. 

The hockey context immediately grabbed attention. Even students who were not die-hard fans quickly became engaged, as the scenario provided an intuitive way to understand abstract analytics concepts. The connection between data, performance metrics, and decision-making became tangible almost instantly.

Student reactions: curiosity turns into engagement

The reaction from students was overwhelmingly positive. What began as curiosity (“We’re analyzing NHL data in class?”) quickly turned into deep engagement. The competitive and playful nature of the game encouraged participation, discussion, and collaboration. Perhaps most importantly, the game lowered the intimidation factor often associated with analytics. Students who initially felt less confident with data became more comfortable experimenting, exploring visualizations, and asking questions. 

What students actually learned

Beyond engagement, the learning outcomes were clear. Students developed core analytics competencies, including data interpretation, KPI analysis, and storytelling with dashboards. Because everything is grounded in SAP Analytics Cloud, students also gained hands-on experience with a professional-grade analytics platform used by thousands of organizations worldwide. This connection between academic learning and real business tools significantly enhanced the perceived relevance of the activity.

The availability of multiple versions (novice and advanced), with different gameplay scenarios (goal scoring, goalies) also allowed us to adapt the experience to different levels, from introductory analytics courses to more advanced classes.

Advice for fellow educators

For educators considering Business Builders – The Hockey Analyst, my main advice is simple: start small and let the game do the work. You don’t need to redesign an entire course to benefit from it. Even a short in-class activity can spark curiosity and meaningful discussions. Encourage students to explore, make mistakes, and reflect on their decisions rather than focusing solely on “winning” the game.

Finally, embrace the storytelling aspect. The real power of this approach lies in how it connects data, technology, and human decision-making within a narrative students care about.

A powerful addition to analytics education

Business Builders – The Hockey Analyst represents a compelling example of how partnerships between academia, industry, and sports organizations (SAP, the NHL, and the ERPsim Lab at HEC Montréal) can produce innovative, engaging, and pedagogically sound learning experiences. For us at Université de Sherbrooke, it has become a valuable tool to enrich analytics education, spark student motivation, and bridge the gap between classroom learning and real-world practice.

-Prof. Olivier Caya, Université de Sherbrooke (Canada)

entry.title: From Dashboards to Decisions: Extending SAP Analytics Cloud with Custom Widgets
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Analytics Cloud is already a powerful platform — and custom widgets are one of the many ways you can extend it to fit your specific business needs. Custom widgets are not a new feature in SAC. What is new is what becomes possible when you pair them with AI tools like Claude Code as your development partner. What used to take weeks can now be built and tested in a fraction of the time.

In this article I walk through four custom widgets built for real planning and analytics use cases: an animated time-series chart, an AI-powered insight panel, a spend pattern analyser, and a resource availability grid. All running inside SAC, all connected to live data.

If you have been curious about what the SAC custom widget framework can actually do — this is the article for you.

Why Custom Widgets?

Most SAC users are familiar with the standard toolkit — charts, tables, input forms, and dashboards. These cover a lot of ground. But every so often you come across a use case that the native components do not quite handle — a specific visualisation, an analytical summary, or a view that your team has to build manually every month because nothing in the platform generates it automatically.

That is where custom widgets come in.

Custom widgets are not a new feature. The framework has been in SAC for a while. What has changed is the tooling around building them. With Claude Code as a development partner, you can go from a business problem to a working widget much faster than before. You do not need to be a JavaScript developer. You need to know what problem you are trying to solve and be able to describe it clearly.

The four widgets below came out of real scenarios. They are working experiments — not polished products. Some things could be improved. But each one demonstrates something that SAC can do when you extend it in the right direction.

 

1. The Animated Line Race Chart with Insights and AI Summary

The Idea

Most time-series charts show you where things ended up. They do not show you how things changed along the way.

If you are comparing revenue across three product categories over ten years, a static line chart gives you the final picture. But it does not tell you when one category overtook another, or how long a particular trend lasted before reversing. That context often matters in planning conversations.

What the Widget Does

The Animated Line Race Chart plays your time-series data as an animation. Multiple lines move across the chart year by year — you can hit play and watch them race, pause at any point, change the speed, or jump to a specific year manually.

As the animation runs, a ranking table updates in real time showing where each entity stands. A KPI strip below the chart tracks growth rates and compounded performance automatically.

There is also an AI Insights tab. Once the animation is done — or paused at any point — you can open it and get a short written summary. The widget passes your current data to an LLM, which reads the numbers and returns a plain-language explanation of what happened: which category led, where the crossovers happened, and what the overall trend looks like.

What Changes

Instead of handing someone a static chart and asking them to draw conclusions, you can walk them through the data year by year. You stop at the moment a trend reversed. You show the ranking shift as it happened. The AI summary gives you a ready-made talking point grounded in the actual numbers. Planning conversations become more specific because the data is easier to follow.

 

2. The AI Insights Widget

The Idea

Looking at a table of actuals versus budget is one thing. Understanding what it means and what to do about it is another. That second step — interpreting the numbers — is where a lot of time gets spent in finance and planning teams.

AI can help with that step. Not by replacing the analyst, but by doing the first-pass interpretation so the analyst can focus on the parts that actually need judgement.

What the Widget Does

The widget is split into two parts.

The first part is the KPI panel. As soon as the widget loads, it reads your data binding and automatically computes key metrics — Actual, Budget, Variance, and Variance % — displayed as summary tiles. No configuration needed. It pulls these directly from whatever data is in scope.

The second part is the AI Insights panel. When you click Generate Insights, the widget passes your live data to an LLM, which returns a structured analysis: top performers, biggest misses, variance extremes, period trends, anomalies, and recommendations. Each point is tied to the actual numbers on screen — it does not generate generic commentary.

What Changes

A planning meeting where people have already read a quick summary of the numbers tends to move faster. Less time working out what happened, more time on what to do.

This widget is also designed to be reusable. The same pattern — read SAC data, pass it to a model, get back a structured insight — can be applied to any metric you are tracking. It is a starting point, not a one-off.

AI Insights Widget

3. The OPEX Insight Panel

The Idea

Finance teams deal with cost centre data in two situations — during planning when you need to understand what an account has been doing before you set a target, and during month-end review when you are checking whether anything unusual happened.

Both involve the same underlying question: what is this account doing, and do I need to pay attention to it right now?

What the Widget Does

The OPEX Insight Panel reads your monthly spend data from SAC and gives you a quick read on each account. It classifies the pattern — stable, growing, declining, or variable — and highlights the months where spend moved significantly above or below the norm.

The output is a short plain-English summary: what the account has been doing, what the range looks like, and which months stood out. A colour-coded label — green, blue, or red — lets you scan across multiple accounts quickly and focus attention where it is needed.

What Changes

During planning, you get the context you need before setting targets — whether an account is stable enough to project forward or variable enough to warrant a closer look. During month-end review, you get a first pass across all accounts without having to manually read through each one. Either way, the time spent on routine checking goes down and attention goes to the accounts that actually need it.

 

4. The Staffing Matrix Widget

The Idea

SAC has strong charting and table capabilities, but a calendar-style availability grid is not part of the native toolkit. For resource planning, that is a meaningful gap. A bar chart or a table can hold the same data, but neither makes it easy to answer the basic question quickly: who is assigned, who is free, and when?

The Staffing Matrix was built specifically to fill that gap — a visualisation that does not exist in SAC by default, purpose-built for how resource data actually needs to be read.

What the Widget Does

The widget displays your team data in a grid — people down the side, dates across the top. Each cell is colour-coded based on the status for that person on that day: green for an active project assignment, orange for leave, yellow for unassigned, and grey for weekends.

The data comes directly from your SAC model, so the grid stays in sync automatically. The output can also be exported to excel with the formatting.

What Changes

You get a view of your resourcing data that SAC does not provide natively. Instead of trying to read availability from a table or piecing it together from multiple reports, you have a single grid where the picture is immediately clear — who is where, across the whole team, across the full date range.

Calendar Widget

Swinlanes

What Building These Showed Me

Building these widgets highlighted something simple but important — most gaps in planning and analytics workflows are not about missing data, but about how that data is presented and interacted with.

Across all four examples, the underlying data already existed in SAC. What was added was a way to:

  • Follow change over time
  • Interpret numbers quickly
  • Spot patterns without manual effort
  • See resource allocation in a format that matches how teams think

Custom widgets make it possible to address these gaps without changing the underlying model. They sit on top of SAC's existing capabilities and reshape how information is consumed.

Another takeaway is how much the development process has changed. With tools like Claude Code, building a first version of a widget is no longer a long technical exercise. It becomes an iterative loop — describe the problem, generate a working version, test it against real data, and refine.

That shift lowers the barrier significantly. It allows business users, functional consultants, and developers to collaborate more closely — with the focus staying on the problem rather than the implementation.

Where to Go from Here

For SAC teams, the opportunity is not just to build more dashboards, but to think more deliberately about the moments where users struggle to interpret or act on data.

Those moments are often good candidates for custom widgets.

A practical way to approach this is:

  • Look for recurring manual steps in your planning or review cycle — rebuilding the same view, copying data into Excel to reshape it, or checking accounts one by one
  • Identify what gets exported to Excel or recreated in PowerPoint before every review — these are often views that SAC can represent more effectively with the right widget
  • Pay attention to where time is spent interpreting the numbers rather than deciding what to do — these are opportunities to make insights more immediate and actionable.

Custom widgets can then be used to complement standard SAC components — not replace them, but extend them in targeted ways.

Over time, this approach shifts SAC from being primarily a reporting and planning layer to a more interactive decision environment, where data is not just displayed but actively explored and discussed.

These examples are early explorations of that idea. There is a lot more that can be done — especially as AI capabilities and custom widget patterns continue to evolve.

A good starting point for this approach is the blog  written by @AdemBaykal  “AI & Claude Code Experiment (Part 2): Building an SAP Analytics Cloud Custom Widget”, which shows how AI tools can be used to build custom widgets in SAC through an AI-assisted workflow.

These are early experiments, not finished products. If you are working on something similar or want to talk through an idea, drop a comment below.

entry.title: Turn tax into strategy: Margin steering with SAP PaPM and SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Transfer pricing rarely gets the spotlight.  But it should.

In this partner webinar, we open the doors to a real SAP journey.  A shift from fixed license rates to a margin-based model powered by Transactional Net Margin Method (TNMM).  The goal was clear.  Align tax, profitability, and compliance with the realities of a cloud business.

You will see how SAP built an end-to-end solution using SAP Profitability and Performance Management, SAP Analytics Cloud, and Settlement Management.  Not theory.  Actual execution.

The result.  Better audit readiness.  Less manual work.  A model that scales as the business grows.

The thing is, margin steering is not just a finance exercise.  It gives your customers a way to stay within arm’s length ranges while keeping profitability predictable.

If you work with EPM, xP&A, or tax-driven transformations, this session will give you practical ideas you can apply fast.

Register now and see how strategy meets execution.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/apr/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013760.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPAnalyticsCloud #EPM #xP&A #TransferPricing

entry.title: From Spreadsheets to Strategic Planning: Smarter Program Budgeting Starts with SAC
entry.content:
For years, Marketing Operations at SAP faced the same challenges every year: navigating a fast-paced budgeting cycle using Excel spreadsheets. Data lived in silos, collaboration was difficult, and leadership lacked real-time visibility into how investment decisions would impact the business. Despite the team’s best efforts, the process wasn’t good enough for the speed and precision marketing requires these days. 

A Clear Need for Transformation 

The marketing budgeting process needs a single source of truth- one centralized place where marketing and financial data can come together. Offline spreadsheets have become very complex, are slow to update, not easy to interpret and can have errors due to manual updates. Teams need transparency and a tool that eliminates manual effort to consolidate datasets. 

Most importantly, there is a need to make quick decisions during planning calls, not hours or days later after analysing the spreadsheets 

Building a Smarter, Faster Planning Environment with SAC 

Leveraging the existing SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), Marketing Operations and CPIT teams joined to design a fully integrated planning module built directly in SAC. 

Using learnings from previous planning cycles, the team recreated the functionality of legacy spreadsheets—then enhanced it using SAC’s capabilities: 

  • Live planning data from core systems, all centralized inside SAC 
  • Data actions, fluid data entry, and currency conversion  
  • Multiple versions to model different scenarios and compare changes  
  • Automated master data sourcing, reducing maintenance and ensuring consistency 
  • A dashboard layer that visualizes changes and tells the story behind the numbers 
  • A data architecture prepared for downstream integrations into CRM and ISP 

The result is a collaborative, real-time planning experience, removing the need to email spreadsheets back and forth or wait for someone to “update the spreadsheet.” 

The Results: Real-Time Planning With Real Impact 

The impact of moving to SAC Planning was immediate: 

  • 94% faster calculations compared to offline spreadsheets 
  • Instant scenario modeling with real-time feedback for leadership 
  • Over 41 marketing attributes blended with financial data for deeper insights 
  • ~480 parameters simplified into three strategic investment levers 
  • Automated budget cascades across 10 organizations, five solution areas, and 8 Marketing Mix types 
  • 100% adoption of SAP running SAP, improving transparency and trust 
  • Elimination of manual imports into visualization tools 

What previously required hours of manual adjustments and spreadsheet now happens in minutes. Leaders can explore scenarios live, evaluate impacts, and align decisions without waiting. 

As M&S CCO Tom Lavin put it: 

“We used our technology to streamline the modeling efforts and make key decisions in real time.” 

This wasn’t just process improvement—it was a full transformation of how Marketing plans investments and responds to change. 

Laying the Foundation for the Future 

With SAC Planning in place, Marketing now has the agility and governance needed to continuously evolve. The new tool brings clarity, consistency, and confidence to planning decisions, setting the stage for broader SAC adoption across the entire Marketing Program Budget Planning process. 

When we start using SAP technology the way it’s meant to be used, we unlock not just efficiency—but smarter, more strategic decision-making.

entry.title: Inside SAP IT 2026: Real Stories, Real Lessons, Real SAP – Live on YouTube
entry.content:
SAP runs SAP

It’s more than a slogan—it’s a commitment. SAP’s own IT organization (CPIT) acts as Customer Zero, adopting and validating SAP innovations before they reach the market. This means every new capability, every architectural shift, and every AI breakthrough is tested first inside one of the world’s most complex enterprise landscapes.

In 2026, we’re taking you behind the scenes again with a new season of Inside SAP IT, our live YouTube series where SAP experts share unfiltered insights, lessons learned, and the real stories behind SAP’s internal transformation.

Authentic. Practical. Unscripted.

 

Whether you’re an IT leader, architect, developer, or business transformation professional, this series gives you a front‑row seat to how SAP builds, scales, and operates the technologies shaping the intelligent, agentic enterprise.

⭐ What to expect from this season

Across ten episodes, SAP IT leaders and experts will unpack how SAP is:

  • Running agentic AI at enterprise scale
  • Modernizing core processes with SAP S/4HANA, SAP BTP, SAP Sales Cloud, SAP CPQ, and SAP Analytics Cloud
  • Operationalizing data products in SAP Business Data Cloud
  • Reinventing support, finance, HR, and revenue operations with AI agents
  • Delivering intelligent experiences through SAP for Me and SAP’s internal platforms
  • Transforming planning, treasury, travel & expense, and more

Each session is live, interactive, and openly shares what worked, what didn’t, and what others can learn from SAP’s journey.

entry.title: AI‑Driven Procurement Analytics with SAP S/4HANA, SAP Analytics Cloud, and Joule
entry.content:
AI‑Driven Procurement Analytics with SAP S/4HANA, SAP Analytics Cloud, and Joule

Procurement teams are under pressure to go beyond static reports and deliver proactive, data‑driven recommendations. SAP now offers an end‑to‑end Business AI stack that can do exactly that  from embedded analytics in SAP S/4HANA to AI‑assisted insights in SAP Analytics Cloud and generative AI with Joule.

This post explains how SAP Business AI supports procurement analytics across the SAP portfolio and how customers can combine native capabilities with external AI where required  fully aligned with SAP’s official strategy and product documentation.

SAP Business AI in a Procurement Context

SAP Business AI is SAP’s integrated suite of AI capabilities delivered across applications and SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP). It is designed to work on reliable business data, follow industry and regulatory standards, and be embedded directly into end‑to‑end processes such as source‑to‑pay.

According to SAP’s Business AI positioning:

  • SAP offers “AI and agents that actually understand all your business processes and data,” with a “single AI interface that seamlessly integrates data and workflows across your SAP and non‑SAP applications.” Also refer to SAP Business AI Roadmaps 
  • Customers can “drive efficiency across every business function with access to over 230 AI-powered scenarios expanding to 400 by the end of 2025,” including finance, supply chain, and procurement use cases.
  • SAP BTP provides AI foundation services and tools to build, run, and integrate advanced AI solutions and agents at scale.

For procurement, these capabilities manifest in three main layers:

  1. Embedded analytics and AI inside SAP S/4HANA and SAP’s spend‑management solutions.
  2. AI‑assisted analytics and planning in SAP Analytics Cloud.
  3. Generative AI and copilots such as Joule, running across the portfolio.

Embedded Analytics and AI in SAP S/4HANA

SAP S/4HANA Cloud includes powerful embedded analytics with SAP Fiori analytical apps, CDS‑based data models, and built‑in AI features. SAP describes these as “AI‑driven interactions” that help users improve productivity and obtain quick insights directly in the operational system.

Key characteristics for procurement:

  • Real‑time visibility on live transactional data from purchasing documents, suppliers, and materials.
  • SAP Fiori analytical apps and KPI tiles for purchasing performance, open items, supplier reliability, and more, which are part of the standard S/4HANA content.
  • Integration into Fiori elements and analytical floor plans that support drill‑down, filtering, and smart visualizations.

These capabilities are ideal for operational and tactical decisions (for example, monitoring overdue POs or supplier delivery performance) and provide a governed, SAP‑delivered starting point for procurement analytics. They can be combined with additional AI services via SAP BTP if customers need more advanced data science workloads.

Official reference: “Enabling new, AI-driven interactions in SAP S/4HANA Cloud with SAP Fiori elements” (SAP asset).

SAP Analytics Cloud: AI‑Assisted Analytics and Planning

SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) is SAP’s strategic solution for analytics and planning and is a central pillar of SAP Business AI for analytics scenarios.

The SAC product page describes how customers can:

  • “Embrace the power of generative AI to automate reporting, discover hidden insights, and develop business plans with Joule copilot.”
  • “Use AI to automatically reveal relationships in data, hidden patterns, and outliers, and to predict potential outcomes and forecasts.”
  • Bring together data from SAP S/4HANA, SAP HANA, SAP Datasphere, and other sources for BI, planning, and predictive analysis in a single experience.

The “Artificial Intelligence for Planning & Analytics” and AI‑assisted features guides describe specific SAC capabilities for business users:

  • Smart Insights, Smart Discovery, Smart Predict  ML‑driven features that help users understand key influencers, detect patterns, and generate predictive forecasts.
  • Search to Insight / Just Ask  natural language query interfaces that allow users to ask questions in plain language and receive visualizations or answers.
  • AI‑assisted features  such as AI‑generated calculations, chart summaries, and explanations integrated into core workflows.

These capabilities are directly integrated into the user interface and can be enabled and governed centrally by administrators. For procurement, this means that category managers or buyers can:

  • Explore spend patterns and outliers using Smart Discovery.
  • Ask natural language questions about spend, supplier performance, or forecasted volumes using Just Ask.
  • Build plans and scenarios with AI‑supported forecasting and explanation features.

Joule: SAP’s Generative AI Copilot

Joule is SAP’s generative AI copilot and is a key element of SAP Business AI. SAP describes Joule as a “single AI interface” that operates across SAP solutions, helping users get more done faster by understanding both business data and processes. For detailed information about Joule capabilities and setup, visit the Joule Help Portal and explore the Joule Community Hub for implementation guidance and best practices.

In analytics and planning, the SAC product page states that customers can use Joule to “automate reporting, discover hidden insights, and develop business plans” by interacting with analytics content using natural language. On the Business AI community pages, SAP highlights that Joule can complete navigational and transactional tasks significantly faster for users across different lines of business.

From a procurement‑analytics perspective, Joule enables scenarios such as:

  • Conversational questions on procurement KPIs (for example, spend by category, supplier performance trends, or contract utilization) with answers grounded in SAC models and S/4HANA data.
  • Narrative summaries of charts, dashboards, and plans, helping stakeholders quickly understand the drivers behind spend or forecast changes.
  • Assistance in creating or adapting analytic content and planning models more efficiently.
  • For a complete list of procurement use cases and implementation details, see the Joule Capabilities Guide for Sourcing and Procurement.

SAP Business AI for Procurement: Spend and Supplier Intelligence

SAP describes “AI in procurement” and “AI for procurement” as a set of Business AI capabilities embedded across SAP’s spend‑management portfolio. According to SAP, these solutions help procurement organizations to:

  • Analyse and optimize spend  AI‑driven spend analysis to identify spending patterns, uncover savings opportunities, and support category strategy decisions.
  • Enhance sourcing and supplier management  evaluate supplier performance, risk, and sustainability and recommend suitable suppliers based on a combination of internal and external data.
  • Improve compliance and risk control  automatically check purchases against contracts and policies, and detect anomalies or suspicious patterns.
  • Automate document processing  use document AI to extract, classify, and validate information from invoices, orders, and contracts, reducing manual effort and errors.

Examples from SAP’s official resources include:

  • “AI in procurement: A complete guide,” which outlines how AI supports spend analysis, sourcing, contract compliance, and supplier risk management.
  • “Discover Advanced AI Procurement Solutions | SAP Business AI,” which summarizes AI‑based spend analytics, supplier insights, and process automation capabilities within SAP’s spend‑management offerings.
  • SAP Document AI, an end‑to‑end intelligent document processing solution that can be used to automate document‑heavy procurement processes (for example, invoice processing).

These domain‑specific AI capabilities provide out‑of‑the‑box value for procurement organizations and are designed to work together with embedded analytics and SAC.

Extending with SAP BTP and AI Foundation

For customers with requirements that go beyond standard content and embedded AI, SAP BTP provides the foundation to build and operate custom AI scenarios.

Key elements include:

  • SAP AI Foundation and AI services  a portfolio of services on SAP BTP (for example, SAP AI Core) to develop, deploy, and manage AI applications.
  • AI units and Business AI Catalog  a consumption‑based model to access SAP Business AI services consistently across SAP solutions, along with a catalog that lists available AI capabilities and their technical and commercial details.
  • Integration with SAP and non‑SAP data  secure connectivity and APIs to combine SAP data with external data sources and expose results back to S/4HANA, SAC, or other applications.

The SAP Business AI onboarding resources describe how organizations can govern AI usage and track AI unit consumption across services. This provides a controlled way to integrate external AI technologies while keeping SAP BTP as the core platform for AI operations and lifecycle management.

Putting It Together: A Reference Architecture for Procurement Analytics

Based on SAP’s official positioning, a typical architecture for AI‑driven procurement analytics with SAP Business AI can look like this:

  1. Operational analytics in SAP S/4HANA
    • Use embedded analytics and AI‑driven Fiori apps on purchasing documents, suppliers, and materials to monitor day‑to‑day performance on live data.
  2. Advanced analytics and planning in SAP Analytics Cloud
    • Connect to S/4HANA, SAP Datasphere, and other sources to perform AI‑assisted analysis (Smart Insights, Smart Discovery, Just Ask) and planning on procurement data.
  3. Generative AI with Joule
    • Enable Joule as a copilot across SAC and other SAP cloud solutions so buyers and category managers can ask natural‑language questions, generate narratives, and automate routine analytics tasks.
  4. Procurement‑specific Business AI
    • Activate AI capabilities within SAP’s spend‑management solutions (for example, AI‑driven spend analysis, supplier risk, document AI) to address domain‑specific scenarios.
  5. Extensions on SAP BTP
    • Use SAP BTP and AI Foundation to develop custom AI models, agents, or integrations where customers want to combine SAP data with external AI services or build very specialized use cases, managed via AI units and the Business AI Catalog.

This architecture keeps SAP as the system of record and governance layer while allowing organizations to add external AI techniques where helpful, always mediated through SAP BTP and SAP Business AI.

How to Get Started

Customers who want to advance their procurement analytics with SAP Business AI can take the following steps, fully aligned with SAP’s guidance:

  • Review the SAP Business AI white paper and product page to understand SAP’s overall AI strategy and how it applies to their landscape.
  • Explore the Business AI Catalog and Business AI use‑case library to identify relevant procurement scenarios and estimate AI unit consumption.
  • Activate and adopt embedded analytics in SAP S/4HANA and AI‑assisted features in SAP Analytics Cloud, including Just Ask and Smart features, for procurement‑related models.
  • Work with their SAP account team and Customer Engagement Executive to clarify entitlements, AI unit model, and roadmap for Joule and procurement‑specific AI services in their region and product portfolio.

With this approach, procurement organizations can move step‑by‑step from static reporting to AI‑assisted insights and, ultimately, to generative AI copilots embedded in their daily workflows  all within the boundaries of SAP’s officially supported platforms and services.

Example : ME2M‑Style Open PO Analysis

Many procurement teams, including yours, routinely analyse outputs from classic reports such as ME2M (open purchase orders) to understand purchasing behaviour, policy exceptions, open commitments, and other operational patterns. SAP’s own guidance on procurement analytics and Business AI maps well to this type of use case.

At a high level, the ME2M‑style analysis could be re‑implemented using SAP’s native layers as follows:

Step / question

SAP capability to use

What it does for ME2M‑type data

“Can we analyse open PO data without exporting to external tools?”

S/4HANA Procurement Analytics and Embedded Analytics (CDS‑based virtual data model and Fiori analytical apps)

Provides real‑time views on purchasing documents and open items using analytical floor plans and overview pages, without data extracts.

“Can we see patterns, anomalies, and drivers in this data?”

SAP Analytics Cloud Smart Discovery, Smart Insights, and predictive features on a ME2M‑equivalent data model

Automatically surfaces key drivers, outliers, and trends in open PO and commitment data modelled from S/4HANA into SAC.

“Can users ask questions in natural language instead of building queries?”

SAP Analytics Cloud “Just Ask” / Search to Insight and Joule copilot

Allows buyers and analysts to ask questions about open POs (for example, by supplier, material, or plant) and get governed, visual answers.

“Can we connect this to more advanced or custom AI if needed?”

SAP BTP AI services and SAP Business AI for Procurement

Enables domain‑specific AI (spend/supplier insights) and side‑by‑side custom models that work on curated procurement data sets.

Concretely, S/4HANA Cloud procurement analytics already provides embedded analytical content (such as the Procurement Overview page, open purchase order‑related tiles, and supplier evaluation) that runs on live procurement data without extraction. This gives you a governed starting point for open PO visibility that covers much of what users currently pull from ME2M.

You can then expose the relevant CDS‑based procurement data into SAP Datasphere or directly into SAP Analytics Cloud and apply AI‑assisted features there. Using Smart Discovery and Smart Insights, teams can look for patterns such as recurring min–max policy breaches, unusual lead times, or atypical price or quantity behaviour in the open PO population.

Finally, with Joule and “Just Ask” connected to SAP Analytics Cloud, buyers can ask natural‑language questions such as “Show me open PO value by supplier with overdue items” or “Which materials have the highest number of min–max violations this month?” and receive answers grounded in the SAC models built on S/4HANA data. For more advanced needs, SAP Business AI for Procurement and SAP BTP‑based AI services provide additional options to detect risk patterns and optimize spend, still using SAP as the system of record.

Information Accuracy

  • Information presented reflects publicly available SAP documentation as of April 2026 and may not reflect the most current product capabilities or availability.
  • Readers should verify current product features, licensing requirements, and regional availability through official SAP channels before making implementation decisions.
